1.synthesis:
Deuteronomy 28 is the covenant's most comprehensive statement of the consequences of loyalty and rebellion, and it opens with blessings. If Israel will diligently obey the LORD their God, observing and doing all his commandments, then the LORD will set them "high above all the nations of the earth" (v. 1). Seven specific blessings follow (vv. 3-6): in city and in field, in the fruit of the womb and the fruit of the ground and livestock, in basket and kneading bowl, in coming in and going out. The blessings are then elaborated: victory over enemies (v. 7), the nations recognizing Israel as the LORD's people (v. 10), abundance of every good thing (vv. 11-12), and the capstone promise — Israel will be the head and not the tail, above and not beneath — if only they obey (vv. 13-14).

Keil and Delitzsch note that this opening promise — "the LORD will make you the highest of all the nations of the earth" — also concluded the previous speech (ch. 26:19) and now serves as the "heading of the following description of the blessing." Matthew Henry observes that the blessings are placed before the curses to indicate God's preference: "It is his delight to bless... That obedience pleases best which comes from a principle of delight in God's goodness." The sevenfold structure of the blessings in verses 3-6 mirrors the seven pairs of curses in verses 16-19, creating a formal covenant antithesis: the same dimensions of life (city/field, entering/going out) are covered by both blessing and curse, depending entirely on Israel's response to the covenant.
1.historical_context: The form of Deuteronomy 28 closely parallels ancient Near Eastern suzerainty treaties, which regularly included both blessings for vassal loyalty and curses for rebellion. Examples from Esarhaddon's vassal treaties (7th century BC) share striking verbal parallels with Deuteronomy 28, though the direction of dependence is debated. What is distinct in Deuteronomy is that the blessing does not merely promise political reward but describes a total transformation of the natural order — rain in season, fruitfulness, protection — as the expression of a personal covenantal God who knows, sees, and responds to his people's faithfulness. Matthew Henry notes that rain in its season was the foundational blessing in a non-irrigated land entirely dependent on the seasonal rains God controls.
1.literary_structure: Verses 1-2 provide the conditional framework ("if you diligently obey... then all these blessings shall come upon you"). Verses 3-6 list seven specific blessings in compressed, antithetical form (city/field, incoming/outgoing, etc.). Verses 7-14 expand each domain of blessing with elaboration. The passage ends with the covenant's pivot verse (v. 13): "The LORD will make you the head and not the tail, above and not below, if you obey," which is echoed negatively in the curses section (v. 44). The blessings are framed as a consequence that "shall come upon you and overtake you" (v. 2) — abundance that cannot be outrun.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry draws the distinction between two motivations for obedience: "it is better that we be allured to that which is good by a filial hope of God's favour than that we be frightened to it by a servile fear of his wrath." The blessings are placed first to appeal to this higher motive. Gill observes that the nations recognizing Israel as "called by the name of the LORD" (v. 10) was itself a form of witness: Israel's blessing was not for its own sake alone but testified to the character of their God. Calvin notes that temporal blessings in this chapter are genuine goods, not spiritual allegories — God created the material world, and its fruitfulness genuinely expresses his covenant favor to those who walk with him.
1.christology: Matthew Henry draws the line from temporal blessings to spiritual realities: the blessings of Deuteronomy 28 are shadows pointing to the "better country" (Heb 11:16). The promise that Israel would be "the head and not the tail" is fulfilled in Christ, who is "the head over all things to the church" (Eph 1:22). The blessing "the LORD will open to you his good treasury, the heavens, to give the rain to your land in its season" (v. 12) anticipates the outpouring of the Spirit in the new covenant (Acts 2; Ezek 36:25-27). In Christ, all the Abrahamic blessings come to the Gentiles (Gal 3:14), and the comprehensive "above all nations" promise finds its eschatological fulfillment in the "every tribe, tongue, people, and nation" of Revelation 5:9.

15.synthesis:
The covenant's curse section dwarfs its blessing counterpart, running from verse 15 to 68 — four times longer. The first major division (vv. 15-46) opens with the mirror image of the blessings: if Israel will not obey, "then all these curses shall come upon you and overtake you" (v. 15). The same domains blessed in verses 3-6 are cursed in verses 16-19. Then the curses elaborate in horrifying detail: confusion and frustration in every undertaking (vv. 20-22), drought — the sky brass, the earth iron (v. 23) — disease, military defeat, the land's produce going to strangers (vv. 24-42), and the reversal of the blessing's promise: "the alien who is among you shall rise higher and higher, and you shall come down lower and lower... He shall be the head, and you shall be the tail" (vv. 43-44).

Matthew Henry observes that "God is slow to anger, but swift to show mercy," and the fact that both blessings and curses are placed before Israel reflects God's patient appeal: "If moderate chastisements do not do the work, God will make them more and more severe" — but always with the aim of repentance. Keil and Delitzsch note that the curses are not for "single breaches of the law, but for contempt of all the laws, amounting to inward contempt of the divine commandments and a breach of the covenant." The curses target not individual failings but a systemic, willful rejection of the covenant relationship. Verse 46 concludes the section with a phrase that explains the purpose of the curses as testimony: "They shall be a sign and a wonder against you and your offspring forever."
15.historical_context: Many of the specific diseases mentioned (v. 27: the boils of Egypt, tumors, scurvy, itch; v. 35: severe inflammation) correspond to conditions well documented in the ancient Near East and represent the reversal of the Exodus healings, in which God promised "none of the diseases of Egypt" (Exod 15:26). The military reversal — "you shall be struck down before your enemies" (v. 25) — was fulfilled progressively in the period of the judges, the Assyrian invasion of 722 BC, and the Babylonian conquest of 586 BC. Matthew Henry identifies the pattern: each successive wave of judgment corresponds to increased covenant violation, with the curses functioning as prophetic history as well as warning.
15.literary_structure: Verses 15-19 are the mirror curse-catalogue matching the blessings of vv. 3-6. Verses 20-44 elaborate the curses in expanding circles: physical disease (vv. 21-22, 27, 35), agricultural failure (vv. 23-24, 38-42), military defeat (vv. 25-26), mental confusion and madness (vv. 28-29), economic stripping (vv. 30-34, 43-44). The literary strategy of reversal is sustained throughout: the blessed basket and kneading bowl of v. 5 become cursed (v. 17); the "head and not tail" of v. 13 becomes "tail and not head" (v. 44). The structure enacts the meaning: every good thing promised can become its opposite under judgment.
15.theological_application: Calvin emphasizes that the severity of the curses demonstrates God's absolute seriousness about the covenant: to treat his statutes lightly is not a minor matter but the rejection of the relationship upon which all national life depends. Matthew Henry notes that "confusion and frustration in everything you undertake" (v. 20) — the Hebrew word is mehumah, a disordering of plans — is the appropriate divine response to a people who have disordered their relationship with God. Gill draws attention to the fact that the diseases in verse 27 are specifically "diseases of Egypt," noting that Egypt itself was under judgment for its treatment of Israel — and now Israel's covenant violation will bring upon them the curses of their former enslaver, reversing their redemption.
15.christology: Paul references the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28 when he writes in Galatians 3:10 that all who rely on law-keeping for justification are under a curse. The curses of Deuteronomy 28 catalog what it looks like when the covenantal relationship with God unravels — diseases, confusion, defeat, stripping, exile. This is the condition of humanity apart from God's grace. Christ, at the cross, underwent the fullness of covenant curse in concentrated form: abandoned by God (Matt 27:46), stripped (John 19:23-24), defeated in appearance, given over to enemies. He absorbed the "sign and wonder" of covenant curse (v. 46) so that those united to him receive instead the covenant blessing that Abraham's descendants inherit by faith (Gal 3:13-14).

47.synthesis:
The covenant curses reach their climax in a graphic and extended description of siege, conquest, and exile. Because Israel "did not serve the LORD your God with joyfulness and gladness of heart, when you had abundance of all things, therefore you shall serve your enemies whom the LORD will send against you" (vv. 47-48). The nation that comes against them is described as swift as an eagle, speaking a foreign tongue (v. 49) — fulfilled in Babylon and again in Rome. The siege of their cities produces such desperate hunger that parents will eat their own children (v. 53-57), a horror recorded as literal history in Lamentations 4:10. The plagues of Egypt return on the survivors (v. 60), and the LORD will scatter them among all peoples "from one end of the earth to the other" (v. 64). In exile, there will be no rest: their hearts will tremble, their eyes will fail with longing for home, and life itself will be uncertain day to day (vv. 65-67). The final verse brings the darkest reversal of all: they will be returned to Egypt in ships, offered for sale as slaves — but there will be no buyer (v. 68).

Matthew Henry identifies this as the most complete description of national catastrophe in the Bible, fulfilled in stages: the Assyrian deportation of 722 BC, the Babylonian conquest of 586 BC, and the Roman destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD. Keil and Delitzsch note the prophetic precision: the details of verse 49 (a distant nation, swift as an eagle, speaking an unknown tongue) describe the Babylonians in Jeremiah's oracles (Jer 4:13; Jer 48:40) and Rome in Josephus's account of the Jewish War.
47.historical_context: The siege descriptions in verses 52-57 correspond to what ancient records preserve of the Babylonian siege of Jerusalem (588-586 BC). Josephus documents that the horrors of verses 53-57 — parents eating children in extremity of famine — were literally fulfilled during the Roman siege of AD 70 (Jewish War 6.3.4). The "return to Egypt in ships" (v. 68) may reference the flight of Jewish refugees to Egypt after the assassination of Gedaliah (Jer 43:5-7), or the Roman slave markets after 70 AD when thousands of Jewish captives were sold but many buyers were unavailable due to the scale. Clarke notes that verse 68 is "the most dreadful of all the curses" — return to slavery in the land of Egypt, the place of original bondage, with even that degradation denied: no one buys them.
47.literary_structure: Verses 47-48 provide the theological hinge: failure of joyful service leads to forced service to enemies. Verses 49-57 describe the siege in two movements: the external enemy (vv. 49-52) and the internal collapse under famine (vv. 53-57). Verses 58-68 enumerate the post-conquest curses: disease (vv. 58-61), reversal of all Abrahamic promise (vv. 62-63), exile among all nations (vv. 64-67), and the return-to-Egypt reversal (v. 68). The entire section is framed as covenant reciprocity: Israel refused to serve God with joy; they will serve enemies without it.
47.theological_application: Gill draws attention to the phrase "because you did not serve the LORD your God with joyfulness and gladness of heart" (v. 47) as a diagnosis of the root failure: not merely formal disobedience but joyless, reluctant service that reveals a heart not genuinely converted to the covenant. Matthew Henry applies this widely: "Those that take no pleasure in the service of God will be made to feel the bitterness of his displeasure." The curse of exile is not merely punitive but restorative in design — Moses will specify in chapter 30 that repentance in exile will trigger return. Calvin observes that even in the darkest judgment God's purposes include the possibility of restoration, as the next chapter will elaborate.
47.christology: The "eagle nation" of verse 49, described as relentless and merciless, bearing the covenant curse upon Israel, is a figure of the judgments that fall on all who reject the covenant Lord. In the ultimate fulfillment, Christ himself bore the full weight of covenant curse — "my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matt 27:46) — so that his people need not bear it themselves. The "no rest for the sole of your foot" (v. 65) and "trembling heart" of exile are the condition of humanity outside covenant peace; the inverse is promised in Christ: "Come to me... and I will give you rest" (Matt 11:28). The worst reversal — return to Egypt in ships as unwanted slaves (v. 68) — pictures the spiritual condition of those who, having known redemption, apostatize; Hebrews 6:4-6 describes the same tragedy in New Testament terms.
